content.assessment: From a public-finance perspective, T1 targets the demand-side capital advantage (F-live-education_finance-02) that lets higher-SES families outcompete for gimnázium seats, but it does not touch the capitation funding structure that penalizes feeder schools (F-live-education_finance-05) nor generates new state expenditure — its costs are largely administrative (exam redesign) rather than fiscal. Value for money is plausible if coaching-resistant design reduces private-market arbitrage, but the underlying resource flow that disadvantages non-selective schools remains fully intact, so the proposal's fiscal footprint is narrow relative to the scale of the problem it targets.
opportunities: Could be paired with targeted subsidy for coaching-resistant test-prep access in disadvantaged feeder schools at marginal cost
Reallocating administrative resources from interview components (already capped at 25%) could be fiscally neutral while shifting incentive structure
strengths: Low fiscal cost since it repurposes an existing centrally-graded exam infrastructure rather than creating new spending lines
Directly addresses the capital/coaching-market mechanism through which SES translates into seat capture (F-live-education_finance-02)
threats: Absent compensatory funding, feeder schools continue to lose both capitation revenue and peer effects regardless of admissions redesign, so the underlying fiscal incentive to poach top students via 6/8-year tracks is undiminished
weaknesses: Leaves the capitation-based funding penalty on feeder schools entirely unaddressed, so the resource-loss incentive structure driving schools to compete for high-performers persists (F-live-education_finance-05)

No fiscal mechanism to reach disadvantaged elementary schools that do not facilitate entry-competition participation at all, meaning zero cost is spent on the non-applicant pool (F-live-education_finance-02)
